title: Why Do Some Soccer Players Cut the Heels Off Their Cleats?
gist: A viral World Cup image of Portugal forward Pedro Neto playing in heel-cut Nike Mercurial cleats reflects a long-standing practice of pros modifying regulation footwear to relieve heel discomfort, often linked to Haglund's syndrome.

insights.claim: Professional soccer players have for years cut the heel sections off their cleats to reduce friction between shoe and heel, a modification that went viral when Portugal forward Pedro Neto did it at the 2026 World Cup.
insights.speaker: Jorge Garay
insights.so_what: Niche insight into how elite athletes customize commercial gear; minimal market signal.
insights.quote: Although the image went viral, it's not new.

insights.claim: Because regulation requires soccer cleats, players cannot swap them out and instead exploit a modification 'loophole' comparable to how they cut their soccer socks.
insights.speaker: Jorge Garay
insights.so_what: Shows regulatory constraints push customization onto stock retail products rather than bespoke gear.
insights.quote: It is a "loophole" similar to the one regarding the cut of soccer socks.
insights.locator: Since soccer cleats are required by regulation

insights.claim: Haglund's syndrome, a bony growth on the back of the heel bone that rubs the Achilles tendon, is among the most cited explanations for the heel discomfort driving World Cup players to modify cleats, though no diagnosis for Neto has been confirmed.
insights.speaker: Jorge Garay
insights.so_what: Context on the medical rationale; not investable.
insights.locator: Among the most frequently cited explanations

insights.claim: The heel-cut cleat that made headlines is a Nike Mercurial Vapor 16 Elite, the same retail model available to consumers but customized by players to fit their feet.
insights.speaker: Jorge Garay
insights.so_what: Minor brand-visibility datapoint for Nike during the World Cup.
insights.quote: The soccer shoe that made headlines for its cut-off heel is a Nike Mercurial Vapor 16 Elite.
insights.locator: In professional soccer, there is no one-size-fits-all
